items.mistake: Copying η from a tutorial without checking loss scale or model size
items.fix: Run a short log-spaced lr sweep on your data; pick η where validation loss decreases smoothly without spikes.
items.why: Gradient magnitudes and curvature differ by architecture, initialization, and loss function—η is not portable.
items.example: η=3e-4 works for one transformer recipe but diverges on a wider MLP; a 5-epoch sweep from 1e-5 to 1e-2 reveals a stable band near 1e-3.

items.mistake: Increasing η when loss plateaus without checking for noise or saturation
items.fix: Plot train vs validation loss, gradient norms, and try schedule decay or smaller η before cranking η up.
items.why: Plateaus can mean you reached a good region, need smaller steps, or have data/bug issues—not always 'too slow learning'.
items.example: Loss flat at 0.4 for 5 epochs with η=0.01 may improve after StepLR(γ=0.1) rather than jumping η to 0.1.

items.mistake: Treating Adam's default η as universally safe
items.fix: Still tune base lr; Adam adapts per-parameter scaling but η sets the overall step magnitude.
items.why: Adaptive methods reduce manual per-direction tuning but do not remove sensitivity to η—divergence still happens with η too high.
items.example: Adam lr=1e-2 on a small CNN may train; the same lr on a deep transformer without warmup can NaN within hundreds of steps.
